questions.question: Which of the following correctly ranks the intermolecular forces present in liquid water from strongest to weakest?

choices.text: London dispersion > hydrogen bonding > dipole-dipole
choices.explanation: In water, hydrogen bonding is the dominant intermolecular force; London dispersion forces are weakest.

choices.text: Hydrogen bonding > dipole-dipole > London dispersion
choices.explanation: Water molecules form strong hydrogen bonds (O–H···O); weaker dipole-dipole interactions and weakest London dispersion forces also exist, but H-bonding dominates.

choices.text: Dipole-dipole > hydrogen bonding > London dispersion
choices.explanation: Hydrogen bonds are a special, stronger subset of dipole interactions; they are stronger than ordinary dipole-dipole forces.

choices.text: London dispersion > dipole-dipole > hydrogen bonding
choices.explanation: This ranking is completely reversed; hydrogen bonding is the strongest type among these three for water molecules.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the boiling point of $\text{HF}$ is higher than that of $\text{HCl}$, even though $\text{HCl}$ has a higher molar mass?

choices.text: $\text{HF}$ forms hydrogen bonds between molecules; $\text{HCl}$ cannot form hydrogen bonds because chlorine is not electronegative enough
choices.explanation: Fluorine's high electronegativity and small size make H–F···F hydrogen bonds strong enough to significantly elevate the boiling point despite the lower molar mass of HF.

choices.text: $\text{HF}$ is more polar than $\text{HCl}$, so it has stronger London dispersion forces
choices.explanation: Greater polarity increases dipole-dipole forces, not London dispersion forces; the key difference is hydrogen bonding in HF, not London dispersion.

choices.text: $\text{HF}$ has a larger dipole moment, which creates stronger ionic bonds between molecules
choices.explanation: Intermolecular forces in HF are not ionic; they are hydrogen bonds and dipole-dipole interactions.

choices.text: $\text{HCl}$ has stronger London dispersion forces because of its larger electron cloud, which should lower its boiling point
choices.explanation: Larger electron clouds do increase London dispersion forces, which would tend to raise the boiling point of HCl, not lower it; however, HF's hydrogen bonding still wins out overall.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Based on the data in the table, which of the following best explains the trend in boiling points for the straight-chain alkane series?

choices.text: As molar mass increases, hydrogen bonding becomes stronger, raising the boiling point
choices.explanation: Alkanes are nonpolar and cannot form hydrogen bonds; the trend is due to increasing London dispersion forces.

choices.text: As molar mass increases, the London dispersion forces become stronger due to larger, more polarizable electron clouds
choices.explanation: Larger molecules have more electrons and greater surface area, allowing stronger temporary dipole fluctuations and therefore stronger London dispersion forces, increasing boiling points.

choices.text: As molar mass increases, dipole-dipole forces increase because alkane molecules become more polar
choices.explanation: Alkanes remain nonpolar regardless of chain length; they cannot have dipole-dipole forces.

choices.text: Boiling point increases because longer chains have stronger covalent bonds that require more energy to break
choices.explanation: Boiling involves overcoming intermolecular forces, not covalent bonds; covalent bonds are not broken when a liquid boils.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Iodine ($\text{I}_2$) is a nonpolar molecule yet is much more soluble in nonpolar $\text{CCl}_4$ than in polar water. Which of the following best explains this observation?

choices.text: Iodine cannot form hydrogen bonds with water, and water molecules prefer to maintain their hydrogen bond network rather than solvate a nonpolar solute
choices.explanation: Water molecules form an extensive hydrogen bond network; incorporating nonpolar I₂ would disrupt this network with no compensating H-bond formation, making dissolution energetically unfavorable.

choices.text: Iodine is too large to fit in the spaces between water molecules
choices.explanation: While iodine is large, size alone does not account for the solubility difference; the type of intermolecular interactions is the primary factor.

choices.text: Iodine reacts chemically with $\text{CCl}_4$ to form a new compound but not with water
choices.explanation: Iodine dissolves physically in CCl₄; the process is solvation by London dispersion forces, not a chemical reaction.

choices.text: The density of iodine is greater than that of water, causing it to sink rather than dissolve
choices.explanation: Density determines sinking/floating but is unrelated to solubility; a dense solid can dissolve in a less dense solvent.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

title.text: Vapor Pressure vs. Mole Fraction (Component A)

questions.question: The graph shows that the observed vapor pressure of a two-component solution (solid line) is higher than the predicted vapor pressure for an ideal solution (dashed line) at all intermediate compositions. Which of the following best explains this behavior?

choices.text: The intermolecular forces between molecules of A and B are stronger than those between like molecules (A–A and B–B), making it harder for molecules to escape into the vapor phase
choices.explanation: Stronger A–B interactions than A–A or B–B would hold molecules in solution more tightly, producing a lower vapor pressure than the ideal prediction — the opposite of what the graph shows.

choices.text: The intermolecular forces between molecules of A and B are weaker than those between like molecules (A–A and B–B), making it easier for molecules to escape into the vapor phase
choices.explanation: When A–B interactions are weaker than A–A and B–B interactions, molecules at the surface are less attracted to their neighbors in the mixture than in the pure liquids, so more molecules escape to the vapor phase — resulting in higher vapor pressure than the ideal prediction.

choices.text: The solution has a higher boiling point than either pure component because adding B to A always raises the boiling point
choices.explanation: Higher observed vapor pressure actually corresponds to a lower boiling point (less heat is needed to reach atmospheric pressure). The premise is also incorrect; boiling point changes depend on the specific IMF interactions, not on mixing in general.

choices.text: Component A and component B form a homogeneous mixture in which the A–B intermolecular forces are identical to the A–A and B–B forces, producing ideal behavior
choices.explanation: If A–B forces equaled A–A and B–B forces, the observed vapor pressure would exactly match the ideal prediction — the dashed line. The solid line is above it, indicating the mixture is non-ideal because A–B forces differ from like–like forces.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: A student dissolves 5.85 g of $\text{NaCl}$ (molar mass = 58.5 g/mol) in enough water to make 500. mL of solution. What is the molarity of the $\text{NaCl}$ solution?

choices.text: 0.100 mol/L
choices.explanation: 0.100 mol/L would result from dividing 0.1 mol by 1.0 L; with 500. mL = 0.500 L, the molarity is 0.200 mol/L.

choices.text: 0.200 mol/L
choices.explanation: Moles of NaCl = 5.85 g ÷ 58.5 g/mol = 0.100 mol. Molarity = 0.100 mol ÷ 0.500 L = 0.200 mol/L.

choices.text: 0.500 mol/L
choices.explanation: 0.500 mol/L would require 0.250 mol in 500. mL; the sample contains only 0.100 mol.
This would require only 0.005 mol; the sample contains 0.100 mol, giving 0.200 mol/L.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of liquids would be expected to be miscible based on the principle 'like dissolves like'?

choices.text: Water and hexane
choices.explanation: Water is polar and hexane is nonpolar; their intermolecular forces are incompatible and they will not mix (immiscible).

choices.text: Ethanol and water
choices.explanation: Ethanol has an –OH group capable of hydrogen bonding with water; their similar polarity and H-bonding capability make them miscible in all proportions.

choices.text: Oil and water
choices.explanation: Oil is nonpolar; water is polar. They are immiscible due to incompatible intermolecular forces.

choices.text: Benzene and water
choices.explanation: Benzene is nonpolar and aromatic; water is polar. They are immiscible.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: The Beer-Lambert Law states that absorbance ($A$) is proportional to the molar absorptivity ($\varepsilon$), path length ($l$), and concentration ($c$): $A = \varepsilon l c$.

questions.question: A student measures the absorbance of three solutions of the same dye in a 1.0 cm cuvette: 0.10 mol/L gives A = 0.23; 0.20 mol/L gives A = 0.46; 0.30 mol/L gives A = 0.69. Which of the following conclusions is best supported by this data?

choices.text: The data support the Beer-Lambert Law because absorbance doubles when concentration doubles
choices.explanation: The data show a linear relationship: A is directly proportional to concentration (0.23 per 0.10 mol/L). This is precisely what Beer-Lambert Law predicts.

choices.text: The data do not follow Beer-Lambert Law because absorbance does not change by a fixed amount per unit concentration
choices.explanation: The absorbance increases by exactly 0.23 for each 0.10 mol/L increase, showing a consistent linear relationship consistent with Beer-Lambert Law.

choices.text: The molar absorptivity cannot be calculated from this data because path length is not given
choices.explanation: Path length is given as 1.0 cm; using $\varepsilon = A/(lc)$, the molar absorptivity can be calculated directly.

choices.text: The data suggest that higher concentrations absorb more light due to increased molecular weight of the solution
choices.explanation: Beer-Lambert Law relates absorbance to concentration, not molecular weight; the increase is due to more absorbing molecules in the light path, not molecular weight change.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the kinetic molecular theory postulate that explains why gases have much lower densities than liquids?

choices.text: Gas molecules move faster than liquid molecules, reducing collision frequency
choices.explanation: Speed alone does not explain density; it is the large average distance between gas molecules that accounts for lower density.

choices.text: Gas molecules are much farther apart than liquid molecules, so gas occupies a much larger volume per unit mass
choices.explanation: KMT postulates that the volume of gas molecules is negligible compared to the container volume; molecules are far apart, giving gases very low densities.

choices.text: Gas molecules are smaller than liquid molecules and therefore take up less space
choices.explanation: The same molecule (e.g., H₂O) can be liquid or gas; the molecules themselves do not change size between phases.

choices.text: Intermolecular forces are stronger in gases, pulling molecules together and reducing density
choices.explanation: Intermolecular forces are negligible between gas molecules; the weak or absent intermolecular forces allow gas molecules to spread apart.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: A sample of ideal gas in a sealed rigid container is heated from 300 K to 600 K. Which of the following correctly predicts the change in pressure?

choices.text: Pressure doubles because pressure is directly proportional to absolute temperature at constant volume
choices.explanation: Gay-Lussac's Law (from the ideal gas law at constant n and V): $P \propto T$. Doubling T from 300 K to 600 K doubles P.

choices.text: Pressure doubles because volume decreases when temperature increases
choices.explanation: The container is rigid (constant volume); volume does not change. The pressure change is due to Gay-Lussac's Law, not Boyle's Law.

choices.text: Pressure remains constant because the number of molecules is constant
choices.explanation: Pressure at constant volume does change with temperature; more kinetic energy means more forceful and frequent wall collisions, increasing pressure.

choices.text: Pressure increases by a factor of 4 because kinetic energy is proportional to $T^2$
choices.explanation: Average kinetic energy is proportional to T (absolute temperature), not T²; pressure at constant volume is directly proportional to T, so doubling T doubles P.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

title.text: Maxwell-Boltzmann: Fraction of Molecules vs. Speed (m/s)

questions.question: The graph shows Maxwell-Boltzmann speed distributions for $\text{N}_2$ and $\text{Xe}$ at the same temperature. Which of the following best explains why the $\text{N}_2$ curve peaks at a higher speed than the $\text{Xe}$ curve?

choices.text: $\text{N}_2$ molecules are smaller and can fit through spaces more easily
choices.explanation: Molecular size is not the relevant factor; it is the mass of the molecules that determines average speed at a given temperature.

choices.text: At the same temperature, all gas molecules have the same average kinetic energy; lighter $\text{N}_2$ molecules must travel faster to have the same kinetic energy as heavier $\text{Xe}$ molecules
choices.explanation: Since $KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ and average KE is equal at the same temperature, lighter molecules have higher average speeds: $v \propto 1/\sqrt{m}$.

choices.text: $\text{N}_2$ is diatomic and has more internal energy from rotation, giving it higher translational speeds
choices.explanation: Rotational energy is separate from translational kinetic energy; the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution of translational speeds is determined by mass, not molecular complexity.

choices.text: $\text{Xe}$ is a noble gas with stronger London dispersion forces, slowing it down
choices.explanation: In the gas phase, intermolecular forces are negligible and do not significantly affect the Maxwell-Boltzmann speed distribution.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following gases would be expected to deviate most from ideal gas behavior at high pressure and low temperature?

choices.text: $\text{H}_2$
choices.explanation: H₂ has very weak London dispersion forces (smallest molecule), making it behave most ideally among the choices.

choices.text: $\text{He}$
choices.explanation: Helium is a noble gas with negligible intermolecular forces, behaving very close to an ideal gas.

choices.text: $\text{NH}_3$
choices.explanation: NH₃ has strong hydrogen bonding (significant intermolecular forces) and a larger molecular volume; at high pressure and low temperature, these factors cause large deviations from ideal gas behavior.

choices.text: $\text{N}_2$
choices.explanation: N₂ has relatively weak London dispersion forces and behaves more ideally than NH₃ under most conditions.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A chemist uses paper chromatography to separate a mixture of food dyes. After developing the chromatogram with a water/ethanol solvent, four colored spots appear at different positions. The solvent front travels 8.0 cm from the baseline, and the centers of the spots are at 6.4 cm, 4.8 cm, 3.2 cm, and 1.6 cm from the baseline.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies which dye has the greatest affinity for the mobile phase (solvent) relative to the stationary phase (paper)?

choices.text: The dye at 1.6 cm, because it traveled the shortest distance
choices.explanation: A spot with a small $R_f$ value moved the least, indicating stronger affinity for the stationary phase (paper), not the mobile phase (solvent).

choices.text: The dye at 6.4 cm, because it has the highest $R_f$ value and moved farthest with the solvent
choices.explanation: $R_f = d_{spot}/d_{solvent} = 6.4/8.0 = 0.80$; the highest $R_f$ indicates the greatest affinity for the mobile phase relative to the stationary phase.

choices.text: All dyes have the same affinity for the mobile phase because they were all carried by the same solvent
choices.explanation: Different affinities for stationary vs. mobile phase are precisely what separate the dyes; if affinities were equal, all spots would be at the same position.

choices.text: The dye at 4.8 cm, because it is in the middle of the chromatogram, representing the average affinity
choices.explanation: Chromatographic separation is not based on averages; the spot that traveled farthest has the greatest affinity for the mobile phase.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: A student uses a spectrophotometer to determine the concentration of a colored solution. The student prepares standards and plots absorbance vs. concentration, obtaining a straight line. Which of the following best describes the purpose of the calibration curve?

choices.text: To verify that the spectrophotometer is producing light of the correct wavelength
choices.explanation: A calibration curve relates absorbance to concentration for a specific solute; it does not verify the wavelength of light produced by the instrument.

choices.text: To establish a linear relationship between absorbance and concentration so that an unknown concentration can be determined from its measured absorbance
choices.explanation: The calibration curve (based on Beer-Lambert Law) allows interpolation: measuring absorbance of an unknown and reading off its concentration from the linear relationship.

choices.text: To determine the molecular weight of the solute by comparing absorbance values
choices.explanation: Spectrophotometry measures concentration, not molecular weight; molecular weight must be known to calculate molar absorptivity.

choices.text: To ensure the solution is homogeneous before measurement
choices.explanation: Homogeneity is ensured by proper mixing before measurement; the calibration curve is used for quantitative analysis.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why surface tension of water is higher than that of most organic liquids?

choices.text: Water molecules are smaller and pack more tightly, increasing surface tension
choices.explanation: While small size contributes, the primary explanation is the strong hydrogen bonding network that creates high cohesive forces at the surface.

choices.text: Water has stronger hydrogen bonding that creates high cohesive forces, making the surface resistant to deformation
choices.explanation: Surface tension arises from the net inward attraction on surface molecules; water's extensive hydrogen bonding creates strong cohesion, resulting in high surface tension.

choices.text: Water is denser than most organic liquids, requiring more force to stretch the surface
choices.explanation: Density and surface tension are not directly correlated; surface tension depends on intermolecular forces, not bulk density.

choices.text: Organic liquids have weaker van der Waals forces, which create stronger surface tension
choices.explanation: Weaker forces would create lower, not higher, surface tension; water's stronger H-bonding gives it higher surface tension than organic liquids with weaker forces.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: An unknown substance is a hard, brittle solid that melts at 1400°C and does not conduct electricity in either solid or liquid form. Based on the table, which type of solid does this substance most likely represent?

choices.text: Ionic solid
choices.explanation: Ionic solids conduct electricity when melted; the unknown substance does not conduct in liquid form, ruling out ionic.

choices.text: Molecular solid
choices.explanation: Molecular solids have low melting points; a melting point of 1400°C is inconsistent with a molecular solid held by weak intermolecular forces.

choices.text: Covalent network solid
choices.explanation: Covalent network solids have very high melting points, do not conduct electricity in any state, and are very hard (though can be brittle); all characteristics match the unknown substance.

choices.text: Metallic solid
choices.explanation: Metallic solids conduct electricity in both solid and liquid form; the unknown does not conduct, eliminating metallic solid.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: A 1.0 mol/L $\text{NaCl}$ solution and a 1.0 mol/L glucose ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$) solution are prepared. Which of the following best explains why the $\text{NaCl}$ solution conducts electricity but the glucose solution does not?

choices.text: $\text{NaCl}$ has a higher molar mass than glucose, giving it more mass to carry electrical charge
choices.explanation: Electrical conductivity in solution depends on the presence of freely moving ions, not on the mass of dissolved particles. Glucose does not produce ions despite having a large molar mass.

choices.text: $\text{NaCl}$ dissociates into $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{Cl}^-$ ions in solution, providing mobile charge carriers; glucose remains as neutral molecules and provides no ions
choices.explanation: Ionic compounds like NaCl dissociate completely in water, producing freely moving ions that carry electrical charge. Molecular compounds like glucose dissolve without producing ions, so no charge is carried and the solution does not conduct electricity.

choices.text: $\text{NaCl}$ is a solid and glucose is a liquid, so NaCl releases more energy when dissolved
choices.explanation: Both NaCl and glucose are solids at room temperature. Conductivity depends on ion production, not on the phase of the solute before dissolving.

choices.text: Glucose molecules are larger and block the flow of current through the solution
choices.explanation: Electrical conductivity in solution depends on the presence of mobile ions, not the size of dissolved molecules. Glucose molecules, regardless of size, do not produce ions and cannot carry electrical current.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: According to kinetic molecular theory, which of the following correctly describes how increasing temperature affects the average speed of gas molecules?

choices.text: Average molecular speed decreases as temperature increases because higher temperatures increase the frequency of collisions that slow molecules down
choices.explanation: The kinetic molecular theory predicts the opposite: higher temperature means greater average kinetic energy and therefore higher average molecular speed. Collisions between ideal gas molecules are elastic and do not reduce average speed.

choices.text: Average molecular speed is proportional to $\sqrt{T}$ because the root-mean-square speed is $v_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3RT}{M}}$
choices.explanation: From the kinetic molecular theory expression $v_{rms} = \sqrt{3RT/M}$, speed is proportional to $\sqrt{T}$. Doubling the temperature (in Kelvin) multiplies the average speed by $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$, not by 2.

choices.text: Average molecular speed is proportional to $T^2$ because kinetic energy is proportional to $T^2$
choices.explanation: Average kinetic energy is proportional to $T$ (not $T^2$): $KE_{avg} = \frac{3}{2}RT$. Since $KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$, speed goes as $\sqrt{T}$, not $T^2$.

choices.text: Average molecular speed is independent of temperature; only molar mass determines molecular speed
choices.explanation: Both temperature and molar mass appear in the KMT speed expression $v_{rms} = \sqrt{3RT/M}$. Molar mass affects speed at a given temperature, but temperature also directly affects speed.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: When $\text{CaCl}_2$ dissolves in water, it produces $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\text{Cl}^-$ ions. Which of the following best explains why $\text{CaCl}_2$ is highly soluble in water but has very low solubility in hexane ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{14}$)?

choices.text: Water is a larger molecule than hexane and can physically surround $\text{CaCl}_2$ more effectively
choices.explanation: Solubility is not determined by molecular size. Hexane molecules are actually larger than water molecules. The key factor is the type of intermolecular forces between solvent and solute.

choices.text: Ion-dipole interactions between water molecules and the $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\text{Cl}^-$ ions provide sufficient energy to overcome the ionic lattice energy; hexane is nonpolar and cannot form such interactions
choices.explanation: Water's polar O–H bonds create ion-dipole interactions with the ionic charges of $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\text{Cl}^-$, releasing enough energy to break apart the crystal lattice. Nonpolar hexane cannot form ion-dipole interactions, so it cannot solvate ions and $\text{CaCl}_2$ does not dissolve.

choices.text: $\text{CaCl}_2$ is covalent in water but ionic in hexane, explaining the difference in solubility
choices.explanation: $\text{CaCl}_2$ is an ionic compound in both solvents. The ionic bonding does not change with solvent. The difference in solubility comes from the ability (or inability) of each solvent to interact with the resulting ions.

choices.text: Water has a higher boiling point than hexane, giving it more energy to break apart the ionic lattice at room temperature
choices.explanation: Boiling point reflects the strength of solvent–solvent interactions, not solvent–solute interactions. The relevant factor is whether the solvent can form strong enough interactions with ions to overcome the lattice energy — which depends on polarity, not boiling point.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: A chemist calculates the number of moles of $\text{CO}_2$ at STP. Which of the following correctly uses the ideal gas law to find the volume occupied by 2.0 mol of $\text{CO}_2$ at STP (T = 273 K, P = 1.00 atm, R = 0.0821 L·atm/mol·K)?

choices.explanation: 11.2 L is the volume for 0.5 mol, not 2.0 mol; using V = nRT/P = (0.5)(0.0821)(273)/1.00 = 11.2 L.
22.4 L is the molar volume for 1.0 mol at STP; for 2.0 mol the volume doubles to 44.8 L.
V = nRT/P = (2.0 mol)(0.0821 L·atm/mol·K)(273 K)/(1.00 atm) = 44.8 L, which is twice the molar volume at STP.
89.6 L would be the volume for 4.0 mol at STP; dividing 89.6 L by 4.0 mol gives 22.4 L/mol, confirming 2.0 mol = 44.8 L.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Based on the data in the table, which of the following best explains why ethanol (78°C) has a higher boiling point than dimethyl ether (−24°C) despite having the same molecular formula ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O}$)?

choices.text: Ethanol has a higher molar mass than dimethyl ether
choices.explanation: They are isomers with the same molecular formula ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O}$) and therefore identical molar mass.

choices.text: Ethanol has an O–H group that can form hydrogen bonds between molecules; dimethyl ether cannot donate hydrogen bonds
choices.explanation: Ethanol's –OH group acts as both hydrogen bond donor and acceptor; dimethyl ether can only accept H-bonds (via oxygen lone pairs) but cannot donate. The additional H-bonding in ethanol dramatically raises its boiling point.

choices.text: Ethanol is a liquid while dimethyl ether is a gas at room temperature, so they cannot be directly compared
choices.explanation: Boiling point is a fixed physical property that can be directly compared between substances regardless of their room-temperature state.

choices.text: Dimethyl ether is symmetric and its dipoles cancel, making it nonpolar
choices.explanation: Dimethyl ether is polar (bent geometry around oxygen); its lower boiling point results from inability to donate H-bonds, not nonpolarity.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: At the same temperature, which of the following correctly compares the average speeds of $\text{H}_2$ molecules ($M = 2$ g/mol) and $\text{O}_2$ molecules ($M = 32$ g/mol)?

choices.text: $\text{H}_2$ molecules move 16 times faster on average than $\text{O}_2$ molecules
choices.explanation: The ratio of speeds is $\sqrt{M_{O_2}/M_{H_2}} = \sqrt{32/2} = \sqrt{16} = 4$, not 16. Speed scales as the square root of the molar mass ratio, not the ratio itself.

choices.text: $\text{H}_2$ molecules move 4 times faster on average than $\text{O}_2$ molecules
choices.explanation: From kinetic molecular theory, $v_{rms} \propto \sqrt{1/M}$ at constant temperature. Therefore $v_{H_2}/v_{O_2} = \sqrt{M_{O_2}/M_{H_2}} = \sqrt{32/2} = \sqrt{16} = 4$. $\text{H}_2$ molecules are 16 times lighter but only 4 times faster.

choices.text: $\text{O}_2$ molecules move faster because larger molecules have more kinetic energy at the same temperature
choices.explanation: At the same temperature, all gas molecules have the same average kinetic energy regardless of mass ($KE_{avg} = \frac{3}{2}RT$). Because $O_2$ is heavier, its molecules must move more slowly to have the same kinetic energy as lighter $\text{H}_2$ molecules.

choices.text: $\text{H}_2$ and $\text{O}_2$ molecules move at the same average speed because temperature determines kinetic energy, not speed
choices.explanation: Temperature determines average kinetic energy ($KE_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$), not average speed. Since kinetic energy is the same but mass differs, speed must differ: lighter $\text{H}_2$ molecules move faster.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student compares the conductivity of three aqueous solutions: 0.1 mol/L $\text{NaCl}$, 0.1 mol/L glucose, and 0.1 mol/L acetic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$). The conductivity meter reads: NaCl = 1.26 mS/cm, acetic acid = 0.052 mS/cm, glucose = 0.0005 mS/cm.

questions.question: Based on the conductivity data, which of the following best classifies the three solutes?

choices.text: $\text{NaCl}$: strong electrolyte; $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$: weak electrolyte; glucose: nonelectrolyte
choices.explanation: NaCl dissociates completely (high conductivity), acetic acid dissociates partially (low but measurable conductivity), and glucose does not produce ions (negligible conductivity).

choices.text: $\text{NaCl}$: strong electrolyte; $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$: nonelectrolyte; glucose: weak electrolyte
choices.explanation: Acetic acid has measurable conductivity, indicating it produces some ions (weak electrolyte); glucose's conductivity is negligible, making it the nonelectrolyte.

choices.text: All three are electrolytes because they all show some conductivity
choices.explanation: Glucose conductivity (0.0005 mS/cm) is essentially zero and attributable to water's autoionization; glucose is classified as a nonelectrolyte.

choices.text: $\text{NaCl}$: weak electrolyte; $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$: strong electrolyte; glucose: nonelectrolyte
choices.explanation: NaCl completely dissociates (strong electrolyte, highest conductivity); acetic acid only partially dissociates (weak electrolyte, much lower conductivity).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly explains why gases at high pressure deviate from ideal gas behavior?

choices.text: At high pressure, gas molecules move faster, increasing the frequency of collisions with the walls
choices.explanation: Increased collision frequency at high pressure does approximate one aspect of gas behavior, but deviation from ideal gas law is due to finite molecular volume becoming significant, not speed.

choices.text: At high pressure, the finite volume of gas molecules becomes a significant fraction of the container volume, causing the actual volume to be greater than predicted by ideal gas law
choices.explanation: Ideal gas law assumes molecules have zero volume. At high pressure, molecules are packed closely and their actual volume is not negligible; the measured volume exceeds the ideal prediction.

choices.text: At high pressure, intermolecular forces become negligible, causing molecules to behave more randomly
choices.explanation: This is reversed; at high pressure, molecules are closer together and intermolecular forces are more significant, increasing deviation from ideal behavior.

choices.text: At high pressure, the mass of gas molecules increases due to compression
choices.explanation: Compression does not change the mass of molecules; the deviation from ideal behavior is due to molecular volume and intermolecular forces, not mass changes.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: A student dissolves 5.85 g of sodium chloride ($\text{NaCl}$, molar mass = 58.5 g/mol) in enough water to make 500 mL of solution. What is the molar concentration of the resulting solution?

choices.explanation: 0.050 M would correspond to 0.050 mol in 1 L, or 0.025 mol in 500 mL. The student has 5.85/58.5 = 0.100 mol in 500 mL, giving 0.200 M.
0.100 mol is the number of moles of NaCl, not the molarity. Molarity = moles ÷ liters = 0.100 mol ÷ 0.500 L = 0.200 M.
Moles of NaCl = 5.85 g ÷ 58.5 g/mol = 0.100 mol. Volume = 500 mL = 0.500 L. Molarity = 0.100 mol ÷ 0.500 L = 0.200 M.
0.400 M would require 0.200 mol NaCl in 0.500 L. The student only has 5.85 g ÷ 58.5 g/mol = 0.100 mol.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Based on the data in the table, which of the following conclusions is best supported about intermolecular forces?

choices.text: Water has a higher boiling point than $\text{GeH}_4$ solely because of its lower molar mass
choices.explanation: Lower molar mass generally correlates with lower (not higher) boiling point via London dispersion. Water's anomalously high boiling point results from strong hydrogen bonding, not its mass.

choices.text: Among the group 14 hydrides, boiling point increases with molar mass because larger molecules have stronger London dispersion forces
choices.explanation: The CH₄ → SiH₄ → GeH₄ series shows increasing boiling points (-161 → -112 → -88°C) correlated with increasing molar mass, consistent with stronger London dispersion forces in larger, more polarizable molecules.

choices.text: NH₃ has a lower boiling point than H₂O because nitrogen forms weaker hydrogen bonds than oxygen
choices.explanation: While N–H···N hydrogen bonds are indeed weaker than O–H···O hydrogen bonds, the complete explanation requires considering how many hydrogen bonds each molecule can form; water can form up to four H-bonds (two donors, two acceptors), while NH₃ has three donors but only one acceptor.

choices.text: All hydrides with molar mass above 30 g/mol have higher boiling points than water because of stronger London dispersion forces
choices.explanation: GeH₄ (77 g/mol) has a boiling point of -88°C, far below water's 100°C; water's strong hydrogen bonding overcomes its lower molar mass advantage.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student places droplets of three liquids — water, ethanol, and hexane — on a glass surface. She observes that water forms a near-spherical bead, ethanol spreads slightly, and hexane spreads completely across the surface.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the differences in spreading behavior?

choices.text: Water beads because it has the lowest density; hexane spreads because it is denser than water
choices.explanation: Hexane (density ~0.66 g/mL) is less dense than water (1.00 g/mL); density does not explain spreading behavior. Surface tension and adhesive vs cohesive forces are the relevant factors.

choices.text: Water has strong cohesive forces (hydrogen bonding) that exceed adhesive forces to glass; hexane has weak London dispersion cohesion that is overcome by adhesive forces to the glass surface
choices.explanation: Water's strong hydrogen bonding creates high surface tension and strong cohesion; molecules prefer to stay together rather than spread. Hexane's weak London dispersion forces mean cohesion is low and adhesive forces to glass dominate, causing spreading.

choices.text: Water is polar and glass is nonpolar, preventing wetting; hexane is nonpolar, matching glass better
choices.explanation: Glass (SiO₂) is polar and actually has greater affinity for polar water than for nonpolar hexane; the beading of water on clean glass is not explained by polarity mismatch.

choices.text: Hexane has a higher vapor pressure than water, which causes it to spread to increase evaporation area
choices.explanation: Vapor pressure does not drive spreading; the spreading is determined by the balance of cohesive (IMF within liquid) and adhesive (IMF between liquid and surface) forces.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Based on the graph, which of the following best explains the trend in boiling points for the diatomic halogens?

choices.text: The halogens become more ionic going down the group, increasing intermolecular attraction
choices.explanation: Diatomic halogens (F₂, Cl₂, Br₂, I₂) are all nonpolar covalent molecules; they do not become ionic going down the group.

choices.text: Boiling point increases because larger, heavier halogen molecules have greater polarizability and therefore stronger London dispersion forces
choices.explanation: All diatomic halogens are nonpolar; their only intermolecular force is London dispersion. Larger molecules with more electrons are more polarizable, inducing stronger instantaneous dipoles and higher boiling points.

choices.text: Boiling point increases because halogen molecules form increasingly strong hydrogen bonds going down the group
choices.explanation: Diatomic halogens cannot form hydrogen bonds; the H-bond donor must be H bonded to N, O, or F. Halogen-halogen molecules experience only London dispersion forces.

choices.text: The trend is due to increasing dipole-dipole forces as electronegativity decreases going down the group
choices.explanation: All diatomic halogens are nonpolar (symmetrical molecules) regardless of electronegativity; they have zero permanent dipole moment and cannot have dipole-dipole interactions.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of substances would be predicted to be miscible (completely soluble in each other) based on the "like dissolves like" principle?

choices.text: Water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$) and hexane ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{14}$)
choices.explanation: Water is polar (hydrogen bonding) and hexane is nonpolar (London dispersion only); their intermolecular forces are incompatible, making them immiscible.

choices.text: Ethanol ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$) and water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$)
choices.explanation: Both ethanol and water are polar and can form hydrogen bonds with each other; the like-dissolves-like principle predicts complete miscibility, which is experimentally confirmed.

choices.text: Motor oil and water
choices.explanation: Motor oil is a nonpolar hydrocarbon mixture; water is polar. These are immiscible because their intermolecular forces (London dispersion vs. hydrogen bonding/dipole-dipole) are incompatible.

choices.text: Sodium chloride and hexane
choices.explanation: NaCl is ionic and dissolves by ion-dipole interactions; hexane is nonpolar and cannot solvate ions effectively. NaCl is insoluble in hexane.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Based on the data in the table, which of the following best explains why glycerol has a much lower vapor pressure than diethyl ether?

choices.text: Glycerol has a higher molar mass than diethyl ether, so its molecules move more slowly and escape the liquid surface less easily
choices.explanation: While molar mass affects molecular speed, the primary explanation for the vast difference is intermolecular force strength. Diethyl ether (MM = 74) and glycerol (MM = 92) are close in mass, but their IMFs differ dramatically.

choices.text: Glycerol has three –OH groups that form extensive hydrogen bonding networks, making it much harder for molecules to escape the liquid phase
choices.explanation: Glycerol has three hydroxyl groups per molecule, enabling extensive hydrogen bonding (each molecule can form up to 6 H-bonds), whereas diethyl ether has only a lone pair acceptor site and no H-bond donor, experiencing only dipole-dipole and London dispersion forces.

choices.text: Diethyl ether is ionic and its ions strongly attract each other, yet still has high vapor pressure
choices.explanation: Diethyl ether is a covalent molecular compound, not ionic; its high vapor pressure results from relatively weak IMFs (mainly London dispersion and weak dipole-dipole).

choices.text: Glycerol is solid at 25°C; solids always have near-zero vapor pressure
choices.explanation: Glycerol is a viscous liquid at 25°C, not a solid (melting point ~18°C); its near-zero vapor pressure is due to exceptionally strong intermolecular forces, not a solid state.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies the type of crystalline solid and its primary structural feature for each substance?

choices.text: $\text{NaCl}$: molecular solid, molecules held by dipole-dipole forces
choices.explanation: NaCl is an ionic solid, not molecular; it consists of Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions in an extended lattice held by electrostatic attractions.

choices.text: $\text{Na}$: metallic solid, cations in a sea of delocalized electrons
choices.explanation: Sodium is a metallic solid; its crystal consists of Na⁺ cations surrounded by delocalized valence electrons that provide conductivity, malleability, and metallic luster.

choices.text: $\text{SiO}_2$: ionic solid, $\text{Si}^{4+}$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$ ions in a lattice
choices.explanation: SiO₂ is a covalent network solid, not ionic; each silicon atom is covalently bonded to four oxygen atoms in an extended three-dimensional network.

choices.text: $\text{CO}_2$: covalent network solid, all carbon and oxygen atoms covalently bonded in a 3D network
choices.explanation: CO₂ is a molecular solid (dry ice) at low temperatures; it consists of discrete CO₂ molecules held by weak London dispersion forces, not a covalent network.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student compares two solutions: Solution A is 1.0 M NaCl in water, and Solution B is 1.0 M glucose ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$) in water. She measures their boiling points.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly compares the solubility behavior of $\text{NaCl}$ and glucose in water, and explains the difference?

choices.text: Both dissolve equally because they are both solid at room temperature
choices.explanation: Physical state before dissolving does not determine how a substance dissolves in water. NaCl and glucose dissolve by fundamentally different mechanisms based on the type of interactions they form with water.

choices.text: NaCl dissolves ionically, producing separate $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{Cl}^-$ ions stabilized by ion-dipole forces; glucose dissolves molecularly, with $\text{OH}$ groups forming hydrogen bonds with water
choices.explanation: NaCl is an ionic compound; when dissolved, the lattice breaks apart and ions are stabilized by ion-dipole interactions with water molecules. Glucose is a polar molecule with many $-\text{OH}$ groups that hydrogen-bond directly to water, allowing it to dissolve without producing ions.

choices.text: Glucose dissolves ionically, producing glucose ions, while NaCl dissolves as intact molecules
choices.explanation: Glucose is a covalent molecular compound that does not ionize in water. It dissolves as intact neutral molecules through hydrogen bonding. NaCl is the ionic compound that dissociates into ions.

choices.text: NaCl dissolves only because it is heated; glucose dissolves only in cold water
choices.explanation: Both NaCl and glucose dissolve readily in water at room temperature. The differences in dissolution mechanism relate to their chemical structure — ionic vs. covalent polar — not temperature requirements.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: The graph shows the vapor pressure of pure water as a function of temperature. At 100°C, the vapor pressure of pure water equals 760 mmHg (1 atm). Based on the kinetic molecular theory, which of the following best explains why the vapor pressure curve rises with increasing temperature?

choices.text: Higher temperature increases the number of water molecules in the liquid, providing more molecules to evaporate
choices.explanation: Temperature does not change the number of molecules in the liquid (the amount of substance is constant). Higher temperature increases the average kinetic energy of molecules, not the number of molecules.

choices.text: Higher temperature shifts the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution so that a greater fraction of molecules have kinetic energy sufficient to escape the liquid surface, increasing vapor pressure
choices.explanation: Vapor pressure depends on how many molecules have enough kinetic energy to overcome the intermolecular forces at the liquid surface. As temperature rises, the distribution shifts to higher energies, and more molecules escape — raising vapor pressure.

choices.text: Higher temperature weakens intermolecular forces within the liquid, causing all molecules to immediately vaporize
choices.explanation: Higher temperature increases molecular energy but does not eliminate intermolecular forces entirely (that would require reaching the boiling point). Vapor pressure increases gradually because only a fraction of high-energy molecules escape at any given temperature.

choices.text: Vapor pressure increases with temperature because atmospheric pressure decreases at higher temperatures
choices.explanation: Atmospheric pressure is independent of temperature for these purposes. Vapor pressure rises because more molecules have sufficient energy to escape the liquid, not because the external pressure changes.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly compares the relative strength of intermolecular forces in $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ versus $\text{H}_2\text{S}$, and predicts which has the higher boiling point?

choices.text: $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ has a higher boiling point because sulfur is larger and has stronger London dispersion forces
choices.explanation: While H₂S has stronger London dispersion forces due to larger electron cloud, H₂O's hydrogen bonding is far stronger (H₂O bp = 100°C vs H₂S bp = -60°C).

choices.text: $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ has a higher boiling point because oxygen's high electronegativity and small size allow strong O–H···O hydrogen bonds that far exceed H₂S's dipole-dipole and dispersion forces
choices.explanation: O–H···O hydrogen bonds (strength ~20 kJ/mol) are much stronger than H₂S's dipole-dipole interactions because sulfur is less electronegative and too large for effective H-bond donation. Water boils at 100°C vs -60°C for H₂S.

choices.text: Both have the same boiling point because both molecules are bent with two lone pairs
choices.explanation: Identical electron geometry does not guarantee similar boiling points; the type and strength of IMFs determine boiling point, and H₂O's hydrogen bonding vastly exceeds H₂S's IMFs.

choices.text: $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ has a higher boiling point because it is a smaller molecule and smaller molecules always boil higher
choices.explanation: Smaller molecules typically have lower boiling points (fewer electrons, weaker London dispersion); H₂O's anomalously high boiling point is due to hydrogen bonding, not its small size per se.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Based on the data in the table, which of the following conclusions about solubility is best supported?

choices.text: Ionic solutes always dissolve regardless of solvent
choices.explanation: Experiment 2 shows NaCl does not dissolve in hexane; ionic solutes require polar solvents that can engage in ion-dipole interactions.

choices.text: Polar/ionic solutes dissolve in polar solvents and nonpolar solutes dissolve in nonpolar solvents, consistent with the 'like dissolves like' principle
choices.explanation: Experiments 1 (ionic in polar water ✓), 2 (ionic in nonpolar hexane ✗), 3 (nonpolar I₂ in polar water — barely), 4 (nonpolar I₂ in nonpolar hexane ✓), and 5 (polar CH₃OH in polar water ✓) all support the like-dissolves-like principle.

choices.text: Methanol dissolves in water because it has a higher molar mass than water
choices.explanation: Methanol dissolves in water because both are polar and methanol can form hydrogen bonds with water, not because of its molar mass.

choices.text: All molecular solutes dissolve in all solvents at room temperature
choices.explanation: Experiment 3 shows I₂ is only slightly soluble in water; molecular solutes do not universally dissolve in all solvents.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: A sample of an unknown pure substance is heated at a constant rate. The temperature versus time graph shows a flat plateau at 78°C. Which of the following best explains this observation?

choices.text: The substance is decomposing at 78°C, absorbing energy in an endothermic reaction
choices.explanation: A plateau in temperature during uniform heating most commonly indicates a phase transition (constant temperature during a phase change), not a chemical decomposition.

choices.text: The substance is undergoing a phase change (likely boiling) at 78°C; energy is absorbed to overcome intermolecular forces rather than increase temperature
choices.explanation: During a phase transition, the temperature remains constant because absorbed heat overcomes intermolecular forces rather than increasing kinetic energy. 78°C is the boiling point of ethanol, consistent with this interpretation.

choices.text: The substance reaches its maximum temperature at 78°C and cannot be heated further
choices.explanation: Pure substances can be heated beyond their boiling points in closed systems; the plateau represents a phase change, after which temperature continues to rise.

choices.text: The substance is a mixture that boils over a temperature range; 78°C is the midpoint
choices.explanation: A plateau at a fixed temperature indicates a pure substance, not a mixture; mixtures boil over a range of temperatures, not at a single sharp temperature.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student uses Beer-Lambert law ($A = \varepsilon l c$) to determine the concentration of a colored solution. The molar absorptivity is $\varepsilon = 8.00 \times 10^3\ \text{L mol}^{-1}\text{cm}^{-1}$, the path length is $l = 1.00\ \text{cm}$, and the measured absorbance is $A = 0.400$.

questions.question: What is the molar concentration of the solution?

choices.text: $5.00 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{mol/L}$
choices.explanation: $c = A / (\varepsilon l) = 0.400 / (8.00 \times 10^3 \times 1.00) = 5.00 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{mol/L}$. Rearranging the Beer-Lambert equation $A = \varepsilon l c$ to solve for $c$ gives the concentration directly.

choices.text: $3.20\ \text{mol/L}$
choices.explanation: $3.20$ would result from multiplying $A \times \varepsilon \times l$ instead of dividing: $c = A / (\varepsilon l)$, not $A \times \varepsilon \times l$.

choices.text: $4.00 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mol/L}$
choices.explanation: This is likely from dividing $A$ by $\varepsilon$ without including the path length: $c = 0.400 / 8000 = 5.00 \times 10^{-5}$, not $4.00 \times 10^{-4}$. Include all terms in the denominator.

choices.text: $0.400\ \text{mol/L}$
choices.explanation: $0.400$ is the absorbance value, not the concentration. Concentration requires dividing the absorbance by the product of molar absorptivity and path length.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why gases are most soluble in liquids at low temperatures?

choices.text: At low temperatures, gas molecules move more slowly and are more easily trapped by solvent molecules
choices.explanation: While slow-moving gas molecules do form more stable interactions, the thermodynamic explanation is that dissolving a gas is exothermic; low temperatures favor the exothermic direction (Le Chatelier's principle).

choices.text: Dissolving a gas in a liquid is an exothermic process; by Le Chatelier's principle, lower temperature shifts the equilibrium toward the dissolved (products) side
choices.explanation: Gas dissolution: $\text{gas} \rightleftharpoons \text{dissolved gas} + \text{heat}$. Lowering temperature shifts equilibrium right, increasing solubility. This is why carbonated drinks go flat when warm.

choices.text: At low temperatures, the solvent contracts and has more space for gas molecules to fit
choices.explanation: Liquids contract slightly on cooling, which would decrease available space; this is not the thermodynamic basis for Henry's law or gas solubility trends.

choices.text: Gas solubility is independent of temperature; only pressure affects how much gas dissolves in a liquid
choices.explanation: Both temperature and pressure affect gas solubility; temperature follows the exothermic dissolution equilibrium, while pressure follows Henry's law ($S = k_H P$).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Based on the data in the table, a student heats an unknown solid and finds that it softens gradually between 150°C and 200°C without a sharp melting point. Which of the following correctly identifies the solid type?

choices.text: Crystalline solid, because all solids melt over a range of temperatures
choices.explanation: The table clearly shows crystalline solids have sharp melting points; gradual softening is characteristic of amorphous solids.

choices.text: Amorphous solid, because the gradual softening over a range is characteristic of disordered particle arrangements
choices.explanation: Amorphous solids lack a regular lattice; particles have varying interaction energies, so they require different amounts of energy to become mobile, resulting in gradual softening rather than a sharp melting point.

choices.text: Ionic solid, because high melting points around 150-200°C are typical of ionic compounds
choices.explanation: Ionic compounds typically melt at much higher temperatures (e.g., NaCl at 801°C) and have sharp melting points; the gradual softening at 150-200°C is inconsistent with an ionic crystal.

choices.text: Covalent network solid, because covalent bonds break gradually over a temperature range
choices.explanation: Covalent network solids (like diamond or quartz) have very high sharp melting points as covalent bonds throughout the network break simultaneously; gradual softening is not their characteristic.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why $\text{NH}_3$ (boiling point $-33°$C) has a much higher boiling point than $\text{PH}_3$ (boiling point $-87°$C), despite $\text{PH}_3$ having a higher molar mass?

choices.text: $\text{NH}_3$ has stronger London dispersion forces because nitrogen has more electrons than the first shell
choices.explanation: PH₃ has more electrons (18 vs 10) and stronger London dispersion forces; NH₃'s higher boiling point is due to hydrogen bonding.

choices.text: $\text{NH}_3$ can form hydrogen bonds (N–H···N) because nitrogen is sufficiently electronegative and the N–H bond is sufficiently polar; $\text{PH}_3$ cannot form hydrogen bonds because phosphorus is not electronegative enough
choices.explanation: Hydrogen bonding requires H bonded to N, O, or F. NH₃ satisfies this; PH₃ does not because phosphorus is less electronegative and the P–H bond is too weakly polar for hydrogen bonding.

choices.text: Both compounds have the same type of intermolecular forces; the difference is due to $\text{NH}_3$'s smaller size
choices.explanation: NH₃ has hydrogen bonding while PH₃ does not; smaller size would predict lower, not higher, boiling point if only London dispersion were involved.

choices.text: $\text{PH}_3$ has weaker bonds within the molecule, making it easier to vaporize
choices.explanation: Boiling point is determined by intermolecular forces (between molecules), not intramolecular bond strength. Breaking P–H or N–H bonds is not required for vaporization.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student uses kinetic molecular theory to model the behavior of an ideal gas. She states: 'At constant temperature, if I double the pressure on a gas, the volume should halve.' She then tests this with real gases at high pressure.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why real gases deviate from the student's prediction at very high pressures?

choices.text: At high pressure, gas molecules gain mass due to compression, violating the ideal gas assumption that molecules have negligible mass
choices.explanation: Compression does not change molecular mass; ideal gas law deviations at high pressure arise from finite molecular volume and intermolecular forces.

choices.text: At high pressure, intermolecular attractions become negligible, causing molecules to behave more ideally
choices.explanation: At high pressure, molecules are very close together and intermolecular forces become more significant, causing greater deviation, not less.

choices.text: At high pressure, the finite volume occupied by gas molecules becomes non-negligible; the actual volume is larger than ideal gas law predicts because molecules cannot fully compress
choices.explanation: Ideal gas law assumes molecules occupy zero volume. At high pressure, the physical volume of molecules themselves becomes a significant fraction of total volume; the gas cannot be compressed as much as predicted, giving a larger-than-expected volume.

choices.text: At high pressure, gas molecules slow down and form a liquid, which has completely different behavior
choices.explanation: Liquefaction requires both high pressure and low enough temperature; at room temperature and very high pressure, the gas deviates from ideal behavior but need not liquefy.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why ionic compounds have higher melting points than molecular solids of comparable formula weight?

choices.text: Ionic compounds have covalent bonds within the crystal that molecular solids lack
choices.explanation: Ionic compounds do not have covalent bonds; they have electrostatic (ionic) attractions between oppositely charged ions arranged in a lattice.

choices.text: Ionic compounds are held together by strong electrostatic attractions between oppositely charged ions throughout the entire lattice, while molecular solids are held together only by weaker intermolecular forces (London dispersion, dipole-dipole, H-bonding)
choices.explanation: The lattice energy of ionic compounds (hundreds to thousands of kJ/mol) far exceeds the intermolecular forces in molecular solids (typically < 50 kJ/mol), requiring much more energy (higher temperature) to disrupt the ionic lattice.

choices.text: Ionic compounds are always larger than molecular solids, so they require more energy to melt
choices.explanation: Size (formula weight) does not determine melting point in a simple way; many large molecular compounds melt at low temperatures while smaller ionic compounds melt at much higher temperatures.

choices.text: Molecular solids have stronger hydrogen bonding than ionic solids, which is why they melt lower
choices.explanation: While some molecular solids have hydrogen bonding, ionic solids are held by electrostatic lattice forces that are generally much stronger than hydrogen bonds (~20 kJ/mol).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: At 100°C and 1.00 atm, water boils. A mountain climber at an altitude where the atmospheric pressure is 0.70 atm tries to cook pasta in boiling water.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains what happens to the boiling point of water at 0.70 atm, and the implication for cooking?

choices.text: Water boils above 100°C at lower pressure; the pasta cooks faster
choices.explanation: Water boils at a lower temperature at reduced pressure (vapor pressure reaches 0.70 atm at a lower temperature); the pasta cooks more slowly, not faster.

choices.text: Water boils below 100°C at 0.70 atm because a lower vapor pressure (0.70 atm) is needed to boil; the lower temperature means food takes longer to cook
choices.explanation: Boiling occurs when vapor pressure equals atmospheric pressure. At 0.70 atm, vapor pressure equals atmospheric pressure at approximately 90°C. Cooking at 90°C instead of 100°C slows chemical reactions in the food (lower temperature).

choices.text: Water does not boil at all at 0.70 atm because the pressure is too low to support boiling
choices.explanation: Water can boil at any pressure; the boiling point simply adjusts. At lower pressure, water boils at a lower temperature.

choices.text: The boiling point remains 100°C because it is a fixed property of pure water
choices.explanation: 100°C is the normal boiling point (at 1 atm); boiling point varies with external pressure. At reduced pressure, water boils at a lower temperature.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: A student uses the data in the table to determine the concentration of an unknown solution by spectrophotometry. Which of the following correctly identifies the concentration of the unknown and explains the reasoning?

choices.text: $0.050\ \text{mol/L}$, because the unknown absorbance is exactly halfway between Standards A and B
choices.explanation: An absorbance of 0.50 falls between Standard B (0.40 at 0.100 mol/L) and Standard C (0.60 at 0.150 mol/L), not between Standards A and B.

choices.text: $0.125\ \text{mol/L}$, because Beer-Lambert law predicts a linear relationship between absorbance and concentration
choices.explanation: The data shows that absorbance is directly proportional to concentration: $A = \varepsilon l c$, with a slope of $0.40/0.100 = 4.0\ \text{L/mol}$ per cm path length. Solving: $c = 0.50 / 4.0 = 0.125\ \text{mol/L}$. This falls between Standards B and C, consistent with the linear calibration.

choices.text: $0.200\ \text{mol/L}$, because each 0.10 increase in absorbance corresponds to 0.10 mol/L
choices.explanation: The data shows that each 0.20 increase in absorbance corresponds to a 0.050 mol/L increase in concentration, not 0.10 mol/L. The linear ratio is $\Delta A / \Delta c = 0.20/0.050 = 4.0$.

choices.text: $0.100\ \text{mol/L}$, because the unknown absorbance is between two standards
choices.explanation: Being between two standards does not mean the concentration equals the lower standard. Beer-Lambert law requires interpolation: $c = 0.50/4.0 = 0.125\ \text{mol/L}$, which is between 0.100 and 0.150 mol/L.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: According to the kinetic molecular theory of gases, which of the following assumptions explains why the pressure of an ideal gas increases when it is heated at constant volume?

choices.text: Heating causes gas molecules to expand in size, creating more collisions with the container walls
choices.explanation: Ideal gas theory assumes molecules have negligible volume that does not change with temperature; the increased pressure from heating comes from more energetic (faster) collisions.

choices.text: Heating increases the average kinetic energy of gas molecules, increasing the frequency and force of collisions with container walls
choices.explanation: By kinetic molecular theory, temperature is directly proportional to average kinetic energy. At higher temperatures, molecules move faster, striking walls more frequently and with greater force, increasing pressure.

choices.text: Heating converts kinetic energy to potential energy, causing molecules to move toward walls more frequently
choices.explanation: Ideal gas theory assumes no intermolecular potential energy; heating increases kinetic energy directly, not by converting from potential energy.

choices.text: Heating increases molecular mass, increasing the force of wall collisions
choices.explanation: Molecular mass does not change with temperature; the increased collision force at higher temperature comes from increased velocity (kinetic energy), not mass.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A researcher studies the viscosity of four liquids at 25°C. She finds the following ranking from most to least viscous: glycerol > water > ethanol > diethyl ether.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why glycerol is significantly more viscous than diethyl ether?

choices.text: Glycerol has a higher molar mass, making its molecules harder to move past each other
choices.explanation: While glycerol (92 g/mol) is heavier than diethyl ether (74 g/mol), the small mass difference does not explain the dramatic viscosity difference; intermolecular forces are the primary factor.

choices.text: Glycerol has three hydroxyl groups that form an extensive hydrogen-bonding network, increasing resistance to flow; diethyl ether has only London dispersion and weak dipole-dipole forces
choices.explanation: Each –OH group in glycerol can both donate and accept hydrogen bonds, creating a highly interconnected network of molecules that strongly resists flow. Diethyl ether has no H-bond donors and only weak intermolecular forces, allowing molecules to flow easily past each other.

choices.text: Glycerol is solid at 25°C; solids are always more viscous than liquids
choices.explanation: Glycerol is a liquid at 25°C (melting point ~18°C); its high viscosity is due to extensive intermolecular hydrogen bonding, not a solid state.

choices.text: Diethyl ether is an ether functional group which always decreases viscosity
choices.explanation: There is no chemical rule that ether groups decrease viscosity; the ether oxygen provides some polarity but no H-bond donation, resulting in relatively weak IMFs and low viscosity.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student is given two gases: $\text{He}$ and $\text{CO}_2$. She is told that $\text{He}$ behaves more ideally than $\text{CO}_2$ under most conditions.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why helium behaves more ideally than carbon dioxide?

choices.text: Helium is a noble gas and noble gases always behave ideally by definition
choices.explanation: Noble gases are not ideal by definition; they can deviate from ideal behavior. Helium approximates ideal behavior because of its small size and very weak London dispersion forces.

choices.text: Helium has a much smaller atomic radius and nearly zero polarizability, resulting in negligible intermolecular forces and negligible molecular volume compared to CO₂
choices.explanation: Ideal gas assumptions are: (1) negligible molecular volume and (2) no intermolecular forces. Helium's tiny size and near-zero polarizability minimize both deviations; CO₂ is larger, more polarizable, and has a quadrupole moment that causes intermolecular interactions.

choices.text: Helium is monatomic, and monatomic gases always satisfy ideal gas law exactly
choices.explanation: Monatomic gases are not automatically ideal; all real gases deviate from ideal behavior. Helium merely deviates less because of its extremely weak IMFs and small size.

choices.text: $\text{CO}_2$ is polar and polarity always causes deviation; $\text{He}$ is nonpolar, making it ideal
choices.explanation: CO₂ is actually nonpolar (linear, symmetric); it deviates from ideal behavior due to London dispersion forces and quadrupole interactions from its large electron cloud, not polarity.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies the strongest intermolecular force present in each substance: $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$, $\text{CH}_3\text{F}$, and $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_3$?

choices.text: $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$: hydrogen bonding; $\text{CH}_3\text{F}$: dipole-dipole; $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_3$: London dispersion
choices.explanation: CH₃OH has an O–H bond, enabling hydrogen bonding. CH₃F has a polar C–F bond with F as lone pair acceptor but no H-bond donor (H is on C, not N/O/F), so its strongest force is dipole-dipole. CH₃CH₃ (ethane) is nonpolar; only London dispersion forces act.

choices.text: $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$: dipole-dipole; $\text{CH}_3\text{F}$: hydrogen bonding; $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_3$: London dispersion
choices.explanation: CH₃OH has an O–H group capable of hydrogen bonding, which is its dominant IMF. CH₃F cannot form hydrogen bonds because the H atoms are bonded to carbon, not to the electronegative F.

choices.text: All three have London dispersion as their only force because they are all organic compounds
choices.explanation: All substances have London dispersion forces, but CH₃OH also has hydrogen bonding and CH₃F also has dipole-dipole forces; these stronger forces dominate over London dispersion for these molecules.

choices.text: $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$: hydrogen bonding; $\text{CH}_3\text{F}$: London dispersion; $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_3$: dipole-dipole
choices.explanation: CH₃F is a polar molecule with a significant dipole moment, so dipole-dipole forces are present and dominant over London dispersion. Ethane (CH₃CH₃) is nonpolar and has only London dispersion.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student measures the absorbance of two colored solutions using a spectrophotometer: Solution X is a 0.10 mol/L solution of a dye and Solution Y is a 0.20 mol/L solution of the same dye. Both samples are placed in 1.00 cm cuvettes.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly predicts the absorbance comparison and explains the difference using Beer-Lambert law?

choices.text: Both solutions have the same absorbance because they use the same cuvette size
choices.explanation: Cuvette path length is the same (1.00 cm) for both, but Beer-Lambert law ($A = \varepsilon lc$) also depends on concentration. Solution Y has twice the concentration, so it absorbs twice as much light.

choices.text: Solution Y has twice the absorbance of Solution X because Beer-Lambert law shows absorbance is directly proportional to concentration
choices.explanation: Beer-Lambert law: $A = \varepsilon lc$. Since $\varepsilon$ and $l$ are the same for both, absorbance is directly proportional to concentration. Solution Y (0.20 mol/L) has twice the concentration of Solution X (0.10 mol/L), so its absorbance is twice as large.

choices.text: Solution X has higher absorbance because lower concentration allows more light to interact with dye molecules
choices.explanation: Lower concentration means fewer dye molecules to absorb light, resulting in lower (not higher) absorbance. According to Beer-Lambert law, absorbance increases with concentration.

choices.text: Solution Y has four times the absorbance because absorbance is proportional to concentration squared
choices.explanation: Beer-Lambert law shows a linear (not squared) relationship between absorbance and concentration: $A = \varepsilon lc$. Doubling the concentration doubles the absorbance, not quadruples it.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly explains why a mixture of two substances can be separated by chromatography?

choices.text: The two substances have different molar masses, so gravity causes the heavier substance to travel less far
choices.explanation: Chromatographic separation depends on differences in intermolecular forces between each substance and the stationary phase — not on molar mass or gravity effects.

choices.text: The two substances have different strengths of interaction with the stationary phase; the substance with weaker interactions travels farther in the mobile phase
choices.explanation: In chromatography, substances partition between the mobile phase (which carries them) and the stationary phase (which retains them). A substance with stronger IMFs to the stationary phase is retained more and travels a shorter distance. Weaker interactions allow the mobile phase to carry it farther.

choices.text: The two substances react chemically with the stationary phase, and the one that reacts faster travels farther
choices.explanation: Chromatography separates based on physical intermolecular forces (adsorption, polarity), not chemical reactions. If substances reacted with the stationary phase, they could not be cleanly separated and recovered.

choices.text: The two substances have different colors, and the stationary phase absorbs light to separate them
choices.explanation: Chromatography does not require colored substances and does not work by light absorption. Separation depends on differential IMF strengths between each component and the stationary vs. mobile phases.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: The table shows the relative average speeds of five gases at 25°C. Which of the following best explains why $\text{H}_2$ molecules move faster on average than $\text{CO}_2$ molecules at the same temperature?

choices.text: $\text{H}_2$ molecules experience stronger intermolecular forces, which accelerate them to higher speeds
choices.explanation: This reverses the relationship. $\text{H}_2$ has weaker IMFs than $\text{CO}_2$ (London dispersion only, very small molecule). IMFs do not cause higher average speeds — IMFs actually slow molecules by attracting them to neighbors.

choices.text: At the same temperature, all gas molecules have the same average kinetic energy; lower-mass molecules must therefore move at higher average speeds to have the same kinetic energy
choices.explanation: Per kinetic molecular theory, average kinetic energy $\frac{1}{2}mv^2$ depends only on temperature. If $T$ is the same, $KE_{avg}$ is the same. Because $\text{H}_2$ ($M = 2$) has much lower mass than $\text{CO}_2$ ($M = 44$), its molecules must move much faster to have equivalent kinetic energy — consistent with the table data.

choices.text: Higher molar mass gases contain more electrons, which repel each other and reduce molecular speed
choices.explanation: Electron–electron repulsion is not the mechanism behind speed differences. The kinetic molecular theory explanation is based solely on the relationship between kinetic energy, mass, and speed — not on electronic structure.

choices.text: $\text{H}_2$ is a diatomic molecule and stores more energy in its bonds, releasing it as translational speed
choices.explanation: $\text{CO}_2$ is also a polyatomic molecule with bonds. Bond energy storage does not determine the average translational speed of gas molecules. The key factor is molar mass: at constant temperature, lower-mass molecules have higher average speeds.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why water has a very high surface tension compared to most other molecular liquids?

choices.text: Water molecules are small, allowing them to pack tightly and create a dense surface layer
choices.explanation: Small size alone does not create high surface tension; it is the strength of intermolecular forces that determines surface tension.

choices.text: Water molecules at the surface experience strong hydrogen bonding only from molecules below and beside them, creating a net inward force that minimizes surface area
choices.explanation: Interior water molecules are hydrogen bonded on all sides. Surface molecules lack hydrogen-bonding partners above them, creating a net inward force. The strong H-bonds (vs. weaker IMFs in other liquids) make this inward force particularly strong, giving water high surface tension.

choices.text: Water is a polar molecule, and all polar molecules have high surface tension
choices.explanation: Polarity alone does not guarantee high surface tension; the strength of intermolecular forces is the key. Many polar molecules have much lower surface tension than water because their IMFs are weaker than H-bonds.

choices.text: Water molecules form covalent bonds with each other at the surface, creating a strong surface layer
choices.explanation: Water molecules form hydrogen bonds with each other, not covalent bonds; the surface tension arises from intermolecular hydrogen bonding, not intramolecular covalent bonding.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
